title: Road running

infobox.value: is the sport of [running](/wiki/Running "Running") on a measured course over an established [road](/wiki/Road "Road"). This differs from [track and field](/wiki/Track_and_field "Track and field") on a regular track and [cross country running](/wiki/Cross_country_running "Cross country running") over natural terrain.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Sport of running on roads

For trains that operate on tracks within a roadway, see [Street running train](/wiki/Street_running_train "Street running train").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Marathon2.jpg/250px-Marathon2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marathon2.jpg)

Road running in a U.S. Air Force marathon

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Bristol_Half_Marathon.jpg/250px-Bristol_Half_Marathon.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bristol_Half_Marathon.jpg)

People taking part in the [Bristol Half Marathon](/wiki/Bristol_Half_Marathon "Bristol Half Marathon")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/1990_Synwell_10-01.jpg/250px-1990_Synwell_10-01.jpg)](/wiki/File:1990_Synwell_10-01.jpg)

Athletes at the start of a 10-mile race in [Gloucestershire](/wiki/Gloucestershire "Gloucestershire") in [England](/wiki/England "England"), [UK](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") in 1990.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Netherlands_Amsterdam_-_Dam-tot-Damloop.jpg/250px-Netherlands_Amsterdam_-_Dam-tot-Damloop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Netherlands_Amsterdam_-_Dam-tot-Damloop.jpg)

The [Dam tot Damloop](/wiki/Dam_tot_Damloop "Dam tot Damloop") is a road race from [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam") to [Zaandam](/wiki/Zaandam "Zaandam") in the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands")

**Road running** is the sport of [running](/wiki/Running "Running") on a measured course over an established [road](/wiki/Road "Road"). This differs from [track and field](/wiki/Track_and_field "Track and field") on a regular track and [cross country running](/wiki/Cross_country_running "Cross country running") over natural terrain.

These events are usually classified as [long-distance](/wiki/Long-distance_running "Long-distance running") according to [athletics](/wiki/Athletics_(sport) "Athletics (sport)") terminology, with races typically ranging from 5 kilometers to 42.2 kilometers in the [marathon](/wiki/Marathon "Marathon"). They may involve large numbers of runners or [wheelchair](/wiki/Wheelchair_racing "Wheelchair racing") entrants. Since the late 2010, some road running events also offer [Nordic Walking](/wiki/Nordic_walking "Nordic walking") as a separate competition along the same route. The four most common [World Athletics](/wiki/World_Athletics "World Athletics") recognized distances for road running events are [5K runs](/wiki/5K_run "5K run"), [10K runs](/wiki/10K_run "10K run"), [half marathons](/wiki/Half_marathon "Half marathon") and marathons.

Running on the road is an alternative surface to running on a trail, track, or treadmill. For many people looking to participate in running as an activity or sport, there are multiple opportunities that can be found on the road.

Road running is one of several forms of road racing, which also includes [road bicycle racing](/wiki/Road_bicycle_racing "Road bicycle racing") and [motor vehicle road racing](/wiki/Road_racing "Road racing").

## Courses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Road_running&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Courses")]

Race courses are usually held on the streets of major cities and towns but can be on any road. [World Athletics](/wiki/World_Athletics "World Athletics") recognizes eleven common distances for road races that are eligible to be counted for records if they meet the eligibility criteria: 1 mile (1,609.3 m), [5 kilometres (3.1 mi)](/wiki/5K_run "5K run"), [10 kilometres (6.2 mi)](/wiki/10K_run "10K run"), [15 kilometres (9.3 mi)](/wiki/15K_run "15K run"), [10 miles (16.1 km)](/wiki/10-mile_run "10-mile run"), [20 kilometres (12.4 mi)](/wiki/20K_run "20K run"), [half marathon](/wiki/Half_marathon "Half marathon") (21.098 km or 13.1 mi), 25 kilometres (15.5 mi), 30 kilometres (19 mi), 35 kilometres (21.7 mi), [marathon](/wiki/Marathon "Marathon") (42.195 km or 26.2 mi), 50 kilometres (31.1 mi), and [100 kilometres (62.1 mi)](/wiki/100K_run "100K run"). The [24-hour run](/wiki/24-hour_run "24-hour run") is also recognized. Of these, the 5K, 10K, 25K, 30K, half marathon, marathon, and 100K are distances that are recognized for world records.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Some major events have unique distances. The [Fifth Avenue Mile](/wiki/Fifth_Avenue_Mile "Fifth Avenue Mile") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") is 1.0 mile (1.6 km); the "Round the Bays" run in [Auckland](/wiki/Auckland "Auckland"), [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") is 8.4 kilometres (5.2 mi); the [Falmouth Road Race](/wiki/Falmouth_Road_Race "Falmouth Road Race") in [Falmouth, Massachusetts](/wiki/Falmouth,_Massachusetts "Falmouth, Massachusetts") is 7.1 miles (11.4 km); the [Manchester Road Race](/wiki/Manchester_Road_Race "Manchester Road Race") in [Manchester, Connecticut](/wiki/Manchester,_Connecticut "Manchester, Connecticut") is 4.75 miles (7.64 km); "[City to Surf](/wiki/City2Surf_(Sydney) "City2Surf (Sydney)")" in [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") is 14 kilometres (8.7 mi); [Honolulu's](/wiki/Honolulu,_Hawaii "Honolulu, Hawaii") "[Great Aloha Run](/wiki/Great_Aloha_Run "Great Aloha Run")" is 8.15 miles (13.12 km); the "[King Island](/wiki/King_Island,_Australia "King Island, Australia") Imperial 20"[[2]](#cite_note-2) is 32 kilometres (20 mi) long; and the "[Charleston Distance Run](/wiki/Charleston_Distance_Run "Charleston Distance Run")" in [Charleston, West Virginia](/wiki/Charleston,_West_Virginia "Charleston, West Virginia") is 15 miles (24 km).[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Participation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Road_running&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Participation")]

Most road running events are open to the general public and attract participants of all skill levels. It is common for large events to draw thousands of runners. Men and women typically compete side by side, and amateur runners often share the course with elite athletes. In more prestigious races, there are usually separate heats for men and women, as well as for professional and non-professional runners. Some events also allow first-time amateurs to compete alongside members of running clubs and world-class athletes.

This inclusive nature has made road racing extremely popular. Millions of people around the world participate in thousands of races annually.[[4]](#cite_note-4) In the United States alone, 18.1 million people registered for recreational road races in 2018.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

While elite athletes can maintain paces of 4–5 minutes per mile (2.5–3 minutes per kilometre), the average pace for recreational runners is approximately 10 minutes per mile (6 minutes per kilometre). The majority of participants run for personal achievement, fitness, or enjoyment rather than competition, and many races accommodate this by remaining open long enough for individuals to jog or walk the distance.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Timing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Road_running&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Timing")]

To accurately record finishing times in road races, organizers typically employ electronic timing systems provided by specialized timing companies. The most commonly used technology is [radio-frequency identification](/wiki/Radio-frequency_identification "Radio-frequency identification") (RFID). RFID tags may be embedded in disposable bibs, attached to shoelaces, or worn as ankle bands. Timing mats placed at the start and finish lines detect each runner’s chip and automatically record their time.

This method is widely considered to be the most efficient and reliable way to track times for large numbers of participants.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Benefits

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Road_running&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Benefits")]

### Diversity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Road_running&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Diversity")]

Road running is recognizable for its diverse features. Anyone is welcome to participate in road running whether it be for recreational activity or for the purpose of competition. Running is an activity that attracts people from all over the world and for any age. For example, many road racing events recognize finishers in an age group system which acts as a way to reward younger or older athletes who may not be able to compete with runners in a prime age.

### Charity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Road_running&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Charity")]

Road races are often community-wide events that highlight or raise money for an issue or project. In the US, [Susan G. Komen](/wiki/Susan_G._Komen "Susan G. Komen")'s [Race for the Cure](/wiki/Race_for_the_Cure "Race for the Cure") is held nationwide to raise [breast cancer](/wiki/Breast_cancer "Breast cancer") awareness. This race is also run in Germany, Italy and Puerto Rico. Similarly, [Race for Life](/wiki/Race_for_Life "Race for Life") holds races throughout the UK to raise money for [Cancer Research UK](/wiki/Cancer_Research_UK "Cancer Research UK"). First person "race reports" frequently appear on the [Dead Runners Society](/wiki/Dead_Runners_Society "Dead Runners Society") [electronic mailing list](/wiki/Electronic_mailing_list "Electronic mailing list"). [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin"), [Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland")'s Women's Mini-Marathon is said to be the largest all-female event of its kind in the world.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### Motivation to be active

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Road_running&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Motivation to be active")]

For many, competing in a local road race can be the motivation needed for individuals to pursue physical activity. In a study done by the United States Bureau for Labor Statistics, road running ranked third in the most common form of sport and exercise activity for Americans.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

### Physical benefit

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Road_running&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Physical benefit")]

Running on the roads has a different effect on the muscles in the human body opposed to running on the treadmill. Treadmills are made to assist running form due to the way the belt pushes your legs back enhancing movement. Running on the road through various conditions such as hills will do more to strengthen glutes, hamstrings, quads, and smaller muscles in the legs.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Additionally, running on the road can help improve bone density as your body breaks down from impact and then regenerates itself.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Disadvantages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Road_running&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Disadvantages")]

### Impact

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Road_running&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Impact")]

As with any type of running, there is a risk of natural wear and tear on the human body due to the different movements required to run. The difference with road running compared to other forms is that for long periods of time, a runner will continuously be landing on a harder surface, which can lead to various overuse injuries. In any given year, on average 65–80 percent of runners experience some type of injury.[[13]](#cite_note-13) In order to decrease the risk of becoming injured from impact on the road, runners can change their shoes every 300–400 miles (500–650 km). This is important because high mileage shoes have poor shock absorption and worn down treads which can cause pain.[[14]](#cite_note-:0-14)

### Danger

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Road_running&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Danger")]

One danger in road running, as opposed to running in any other location, is that vehicles drive by regularly at high speeds. Runners can take precautions to decrease this risk, including: wearing reflective gear, wearing bright colors, running only during daylight, wearing a headlight, and running on the side of the road opposite traffic.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*][*[facing oncoming traffic?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Cleanup "Wikipedia:Cleanup")*]

### Costs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Road_running&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Costs")]

Although running can seem like a very cheap activity that anyone can partake in, there are some expenses. Replacing running shoes every 400–500 miles (650–800 km) costs money.[[14]](#cite_note-:0-14)[*[It was 300 to 400 miles earlier in the article.](/wiki/Wikipedia:Cleanup "Wikipedia:Cleanup")*] Also, road race entry fees can be expensive because they have to cover the costs incurred by the race organizer. The entry fee for famous marathons across the world can be considerable.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## Governing body and international organizations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Road_running&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Governing body and international organizations")]

The international governing body for road racing is [World Athletics](/wiki/World_Athletics "World Athletics") (formerly IAAF). World Athletics aims to set the standards for competitions by ensuring that all participants are drug-free and that all equipment used is legal.[[16]](#cite_note-16) World Athletics measures each race course to give it an World Athletics certification rating. Once a race course is certified, the course can be counted for different records or rankings.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

National governing bodies which are affiliated to World Athletics are responsible for road races held in their country. Of the thousands of road races held each year, 238 races, including some premier ones, are members of the [Association of International Marathons and Distance Races](/wiki/Association_of_International_Marathons_and_Distance_Races "Association of International Marathons and Distance Races") (AIMS). Many race organizers (or the running clubs which conduct the races) are members of the [Road Runners Club of America](/wiki/Road_Runners_Club_of_America "Road Runners Club of America"). In addition, the USA Track & Field plays a role in selecting representatives for certain international competitions under the [Amateur Sports Act of 1978](/wiki/Amateur_Sports_Act_of_1978 "Amateur Sports Act of 1978").

Competitors from around the world participate in what are dubbed the "elite" races for cash prizes. Elite level road running series include the [World Marathon Majors](/wiki/World_Marathon_Majors "World Marathon Majors"), the [Great Run](/wiki/Great_Run "Great Run") series, and [IAAF Road Race Label Events](/wiki/IAAF_Road_Race_Label_Events "IAAF Road Race Label Events").

## Main competitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Road_running&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Main competitions")]

### Marathon and half marathon events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Road_running&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Marathon and half marathon events")]

* [World Marathon Cup](/wiki/World_Marathon_Cup "World Marathon Cup")

* [European Marathon Cup](/wiki/European_Marathon_Cup "European Marathon Cup")

### Race-walking events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Road_running&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Race-walking events")]

* [World Race Walking Team Championships](/wiki/World_Race_Walking_Team_Championships "World Race Walking Team Championships")
* [European Race Walking Cup](/wiki/European_Race_Walking_Cup "European Race Walking Cup")
